I have a special validator class. The code is as follows:
```#!/usr/bin/env python3

import datetime
from connexion.decorators.validation import RequestBodyValidator
from jsonschema import ValidationError
from connexion import problem
from flask import Flask

app = Flask(__name__)
BAD_REQUEST = 'Bad Request'

class RequestBodyValidatorVerboseErrorLogging(RequestBodyValidator):
    
    def validate_schema(self, data, url):
        if self.is_null_value_valid and is_null(data):
            return None

        try:
            self.validator.validate(data)
        except ValidationError as exception:
            app.logger.error("{timestamp} {url} VALIDATION_ERROR: {error} REQUEST_BODY: {request_body}".format(timestamp=datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),url=url, error=exception.message, request_body=data))
            return problem(400, BAD_REQUEST, str(exception.message))

        return None
```

I want to log the error messages of this class to a rotating file. Earlier, I was using the common logger namespace convention but my suspicion was that the Flask application was taking precedence. I changed the logging to use `app.logger.error` instead of `logger.error`. Notice my import above.

My main API file is as follows:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import connexion
import logging
import sys
from event_api_validators import RequestBodyValidatorVerboseErrorLogging
from logging.handlers import RotatingFileHandler

SWAGGER_PATH = '/event_api/'
LOG_PATH = '/error_logs/event_api_error_log.log'
RESOLVED_SWAGGER_FILE = 'api_index_resolved.json'

log_handler = RotatingFileHandler(LOG_PATH, mode='a', maxBytes=100*1024*1024, 
                                 backupCount=10, encoding=None, delay=False)
log_handler.setLevel(logging.ERROR)

validator_map = {
    'body': RequestBodyValidatorVerboseErrorLogging
}

event_api = connexion.App(__name__, validator_map=validator_map)
event_api.app.logger.addHandler(log_handler)
event_api.app.logger.setLevel(logging.ERROR)
event_api.add_api(RESOLVED_SWAGGER_FILE, swagger_path=SWAGGER_PATH)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    event_api.run(port=80)
```

This works with the `RotatingFileHandler`. Additionally, I still write to stream with the default log handler as well.

After playing around a bit more I figured out what was going on.  If you *do not* have "hide on lose focus" checked (in the settings) then the Guake still "hides" when you lose focus on it, in the sense that it gets hidden behind the window you did focus on.  But as far as Guake is concerned it's not "hidden", so when you hit F12 it doesn't appear ... because it thought you wanted to hide it.  Hitting F12 a second time then reveals it.

Obviously this is user error.  But perhaps Guake could be a little more helpful by warning you when this happens?  In other words, if Guake is open, but covered by another window, and you hit F12, Guake could pop up a tiny message to the effect of "You just hid Guake when it was already behind another window.  If this is not what you intended, please enable the 'hide on lose focus setting'."

Since some people may like this behavior, you could make the message one of those "click here to never show  this message again" messages.  This way those users just have to click away that message a single time, but users who are confused by the behavior could be "corrected" by Guake.

Then again, maybe you don't want to put that much effort in to stopping users from doing stupid things to themselves ... in which case hopefully this issue will serve as an explanation when those users google for a solution :)

Bonjour

Il est possible d'utiliser les références ELI (European Legislation Identifier) pour référencer des textes ou des articles de texte sur Légifrance. Ces références peuvent la plupart du temps se calculer à partir de la référence législative dans les fichiers XLS des barêmes IPP :

Pour prendre un exemple, dans https://www.ipp.eu/outils/baremes-ipp/prelevements-sociaux/, onglet "CSG-ACTIVITE", ligne 5 : "Loi 2010-1594 du 20/12/2010, art. 20 (LFSS pour 2011)" se transforme assez directement en https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/eli/loi/2010/12/20/2010-1594/jo/article_20, qui donne directement accès à l'article. La forme des références ELI pour Legifrance, documentée à http://www.eli.fr/fr/constructionURI.html, est ..../eli/{type du texte}/{annee}/{mois}/{jour}/{numéro de texte}/jo/article_{numero}. On peut omettre la dernière partie "article_xxx" pour avoir accès au texte entier (https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/eli/loi/2010/12/20/2010-1594/jo).

Si les colonnes du tableau Excel sont remplies avec des références juridiques écrites d'une façon cohérente, une série d'expressions régulières ou d'extraction simples devraient permettre de générer les ELI correspondants. On peut ensuite faire une seconde passe pour tester que les URI générées ne renvoient pas une erreur.

Some high level thoughts:

- before https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/pull/521 the Linux/traditional binary analysis API was the primary and only Manticore API
- #521 was merged, theoretically adding an EVM API, but in an imbalanced and kind of half baked way. The EVM API does not reside in the actual manticore package. 
- The EVM API and Linux APIs are quite different, because they are designed for analysis of two very different models of execution (smart contract/blockchain execution environment vs traditional hardware binary execution environment)
- It may be and is probably possible to design one super amazing API that is somehow generic enough to fit both of these execution models. It will probably be a lot of work, and pretty hard to do this.
  - [this](https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/pull/421#issuecomment-320115817) was my initial attempt at designing such a generic API. it was implemented, but is not a good solution because the EVM API scripts that ultimately exist now cannot be easily ported to it. This is because the concept of a ManticoreEVM API object does not exist in that solution.
- For the moment, let's aim to shoot somewhere in between. We can seek to provide two clean, well designed, and usable APIs that are largely separate, but simply both reside in the manticore package. I believe something like https://github.com/twisted/twisted could be good influence, their API is structured around a large collection of somewhat separate submodules
I'm just merging this considering it's a development branch and not master.

useFixture bugs[1] have been created to avoid different  behavior for testtools/fixtures addCleanup. This change solves them by introducing a new difference: unittest/testtools setUp VS fixtures _setUp

Moreover i would prefer this change raises a warning if setUp is overloaded (perhaps i miss it) as it's natural to overload setUp (unittest behavior) not _setUp: fixture developers could/should update their code (as the change does for fixtures in fixtures package).


[1] https://bugs.launchpad.net/testtools/+bug/1456370
[1] https://bugs.launchpad.net/python-fixtures/+bug/1456353
[2] currently testtools calls cleanups even if setUp fails, fixtures only if setUp succeed

Places which touch `client.project`:
- `list_buckets` is the only client method which uses the project:  I guess we could add an optional `project` argument, falling back to the client's project.  Likely we should raise an exception if neither is set: `project` is a [required parameter for 'buckets.list'](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/buckets/list#project).
- `Bucket.create` is the only bucket method which uses it: it is [likewise required](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/buckets/insert#project), so we could allow an optional `project` argument and fall back / fail in the same way.  We would also need to plumb it through to `Client.create_bucket`.
- `BucketNotification.__init__` takes and optional `topic_project` parameter, and falls back to the client's project.  We would need to have it raise if neither was set.

To implement this, we could have the default for the `project` argument be a marker, and then treat `None` specially (pass through a dummy to the `super()` version and then assign `None`.

Thanks for raising this, it's a very real issue.

tl;dr: I think we can reduce this by about 15 minutes with work I'm already doing, and one change.

The Rust side has the majority of the recent additions here. Some break-downs:

13 minutes was spent fetching dependencies. This was mostly because we have a forked version of grpcio right now, so it has to do a whole git clone of the repo (with a *lot* of submodules), rather than just fetching a release blob from cargo. I'm in the process of upstreaming the patches in my fork, so that should hopefully go away this week. The total fetch time for all dependencies should be around one minute after that upstreaming is done. Fixing https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/issues/4975 may require another patch-set to be upstreamed, though (and similarly for each issue we need to fix).

7 minutes was spent building an optimised release version of the native engine. We should probably make a non-optimised debug version the default, and only create release binaries when we're explicitly releasing. The difference on my 13" MacBook Pro is about 4 minutes:
```
$ rm -rf ~/.cache/pants/bin/native-engine $(find . -name target)
$ time cargo build
<snip>
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 141.16 secs

real	2m22.858s
user	3m54.210s
sys	0m49.880s

$ rm -rf ~/.cache/pants/bin/native-engine $(find . -name target)
$ time cargo build --release
<snip>
Finished release [optimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 391.30 secs

real	6m33.019s
```

I guess there should also be a discussion about whether we set a hard limit and reject PRs that push us over that.

I think implementing more famous published models can really help improve the community of framework and toolkits like sockeye. GNMT is one of this kind of famous models. So we are trying to implement and train it on the top of mxnet and sockeye. I know GluonNLP is also trying to do something similar.
Here in this PR, we want to present a GNMT model as close as possible to the original paper, and also want to make it comparable to the TF implementation for both accuracy and performance. As @xinyu-intel mentioned, we have got 26.91 for BLEU on both CPU and GPU. If you are interested in the performance of this model, I think @xinyu-intel can also share some initial data for it. Anyway, I think it would be great if we can figure out that mxnet and sockeye have the same GNMT model which has better performance compared with other frameworks, right?
So if you find anything in this PR does not align with the original GNMT paper or TF implementation, please kindly point it out. Or if you have a more general implementation for GNMT, please kindly share it with us if possible. We are pleased and looking forward to working with the sockeye community to make this happen.

I just meant, that I don't think it is screamingly obvious, when reading through the docs https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.debug, https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.handlers.html#filehandler etc. exactly what types logging expects and what happens to it on the way through the machinery. BTW, the format into binary string does not seem to work on python3. If you do this:
```python
logging.warning(b'And this, too %s', b'uu')
```
you get this `WARNING:root:b'And this, too b'uu''`. But I will figure that out.

In any case. I agree with your points and it will be a better implementation if we use the logs and move everything into the tool. I will close this and get hacking.

Ok. So I have now dug a little more into the situation with the favorite radio shows. It turns out, because radio station support is this mish mash of built in Sonos support and TuneIn, that when you need to browse the radio favorite shows (which is not playable)[1] to get to the actual shows, then you need to use the TuneIn plugin. And when doing that, you probably need to extract the proper TuneIn ID from the URI. So I think a more proper implementation of this has to be considered. Possibly when/if moving radio stuff into its own module, this back and forth between Sonos built in stuff and plugins can be implemented there or maybe the plugin can do everything and we can avoid the shift. For now however, I'm satisfied with the state it is in. I have replaced the return types with data structures, which I will want to do anyway. The returned object for the favorite shows cannot be used for anything except information, but that is (as far as I can tell) not different from the way it was before, so no harm done. So for now, instead of fixing the deficiencies, I have merely documented them in the docstring.

[1] If you try the Sonos app, you will notice that when (in the music tree) you enter the favorite shows. The items listed there are not playable, when browsing those, you get into a single item with "Current shows" and only when browsing that do you get to a list of actual broadcasts, which can be played.

As a followup on @ibnesayeed's suggestion, `signal.ALRM` on Windows produces an `AttributeError`. On Mac (and I presume Linux, untested), `signal.SIGALRM` returns an integer.

Similarly, `signal.SIGABRT` produces an integer on both Windows and Mac, so we may be able to try-catch on the call to `signal.SIGALRM` instead of explicitly conditioning on the Windows OS.

This check ought to be moot once we have the threading working correctly to re-enable the timeout functionality for Windows, but I wanted to follow-up on the previous message with the suboptimal OS-check solution.

One problem is that for the NP-Hard algorithms (e.g. maximum independent set) the verification function is itself NP-Hard. This means that we would be verifying some subset of the algorithm. Continuing with the example of maximum independent set, we could either check that it is a _maximal_ independent set, or just that it is an independent set. Because it is not obvious which we would check, this would violate the principle of least astonishment.

This is why we have provided explicit verification functions like [is_independent_set](https://dwave-networkx.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/algorithms/generated/dwave_networkx.is_independent_set.html#dwave_networkx.is_independent_set) and specified in the documentation that the solution may not be correct. This way, the user knows exactly what they have verified and if the verification is expensive, the user has actively opted in.

If there are additional verification functions you would like, please feel free to make a feature request.

@danielballan: Good point about turning on debug messages. I'm actually leaning toward an API call that covers common cases. I think we expect many `trackpy` functions to produce useful debug messages, and we'll want to mention this in each function's docstring or tutorial. In those places, it would be great to refer users to a single function, instead of a separate tutorial on how to configure logging. 

`quiet()` is nice because it lines up a logging facility feature with a very clear user intent. But here, we have a few problems:

1. The IPython hook to clear the most recent message is going to be confusing and counter-productive when debug output is turned on. (This fact alone means that simply setting the logging level will not suffice.)
- Especially for a short movie or operation, one would probably prefer to see the debug output scroll by in the interactive session.
- For a long movie that runs unattended, it would be best to send the debug messages to a file, while still viewing progress updates in the console.

In other words, here user intents are less obvious. Even though the Python logging facility can address all of these cases, creating a user-friendly API solution could just be overly complicated. If we were to create such an API, I would propose something like:

- `trackpy.debug(True)` (default) — turn off the clear-output hook (if IPython). Set stdout level to DEBUG
- `trackpy.debug(file-like object)` — create a separate logging output to this file
- `trackpy.debug(False)` — turn on the clear-output hook, set stdout level to INFO, and close the output file

Note that only `debug(False)` returns the logging facility to a known state. Note also that `debug(False)` would *cancel* an earlier `quiet(True)` call. This could be confusing. A unified `trackpy.progress()` or `trackpy.log()` function might be better.

Unless everyone agrees that this kind of solution is compelling, it might be wise to save it for a separate PR (that I volunteer to try writing), so that we can try it out with actual code that makes debug messages, and consider the alternative of just instructing the user on how to re-configure the logging facility.

other way around. The CI system (and ONLY the CI system in principle)
should know the private key, while deis would need the public key.

In general I am intrested in more fine-grained ACL's to apps, specifically
I am intrested in following options

* (As this use case): a (pseude)-user that can do deployments
* A user to manage domains / certs
* An admin that can do everything.

Reason I am intrested in second bullet point is that I have a website where
people (in the future) should be able to subscribe to a SaaS system, and
choose their own domain name attached to it.
At that point a very restricted user should be able to access the DEIS API,
and add domains to the app.

Ideally containers would run on an isolated-by-default network segment with explicit access grants to endpoints as permitted (e.g. other containers, control plane infrastructure and external/third-party services).  Unfortunately, achieving this level of network security is non-trivial and we want to be open about that, hence the disclosure in our documentation.

I do want to be clear that there a big difference between running untrusted code _by design_ and running it _by accident_ via application compromise.  While the net result is the same (untrusted code running inside the cluster), the latter scenario is no different in Deis than what you'll find on comparable container platforms or orchestration systems like Mesos, Kubernetes, etc.

We do intend to support multi-tenant environments eventually.  We are exploring technologies along the lines of [ambassadord](https://github.com/progrium/ambassadord) and Apcera's [semantic pipelines](http://docs.apcera.com/tutorials/pipelines/) (not open source) to help us get there.  We also need help from upstream projects like Docker and etcd.  However, until we undergo an audit by a 3rd-party security vendor, it's unlikely we will remove the disclaimer re: true multi-tenancy.

It's actually much worse than that,. The package **is** being removed - if you try importing it in python, it will fail. However the fact that it has been removed is **not** recognized by `pip`. If you try `pip freeze`, it **will** list the package. As a result, anything you try to do with pip is going to be completely broken (and you do still need pip to manage pure python dependencies if you want a decent build process).

To state it again, for whatever reason, when `conda env update --file environment.yml --prune` command deletes a conda package which is no longer listed in requirements,, it does not delete some metadata which makes `pip` think the package is still there. This is incredibly annoying... I would love to see a fix for that!

So you are actually looking for the the maximum EI value possible? For that you need the mean and variance prediction of the forest which involve all trees. Furthermore, EI is a non-stationary quantity, meaning the values you can achieve change over time. For a model with a lot of points that is certain about the optimum, the EI value are much smaller compared to a model trained with less data. So do be careful with comparing those.

I went through the debugger a bunch and found that `verify_certs` is ignored if `ca_certs` is `None` or set to some value (`None` is taken as "use defaults", which results in certs being set to required). Simply set this to a `False` value of some sort that isn't `None` and it should work.

```
es = Elasticsearch("https://user:pass@myelasticsearch",
                   ca_certs=False,
                   verify_certs=False)
```

This seems to be an issue with the underlying Python library, but it's difficult to figure that out due to the way keyword args are passed around in the Elasticsearch library.

Group
    az network watcher flow-log: (PREVIEW) Commands to manage NSG flow logging.

    configure: Configure flow logging on a Network Security Group (NSG).
    show     : Show flow log configuration for an NSG.
```

**Network Watcher packet-capture Group**
```
Group
    az network watcher packet-capture: (PREVIEW) Commands to manage packet capture sessions on VMs.
        Requires that Network Watcher is enabled for the region in which the VM is located
        and that the AzureNetworkWatcherExtension is enabled on the VM.

Commands:
    create     : Create and start a packet capture session.
    delete     : Deletes the specified packet capture session.
    list       : Lists all packet capture sessions within the specified resource group.
    show       : Gets a packet capture session by name.
    show-status: Query the status of a running packet capture session.
    stop       : Stops a specified packet capture session.
```

Group
    az network watcher troubleshooting: (PREVIEW) Commands to managed Network Watcher

    show : Show results of the last troubleshooting operation.
    start: Troubleshoot issues with VPN connections or gateway connectivity.

2. I don't really have any interest in maintaining `conda` support -- the complexity is not worth it imo.  There's far more edge cases with `conda` than there are with `virtualenv`.  conda also cannot be distributed with `pre-commit` itself so it's hard to make it work out of the box.

That said, if you want to hack on a `conda` integration -- I'd suggest taking a look at the [`python` language](https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/blob/master/pre_commit/languages/python.py) -- it would probably make the most sense to implement `language: conda` separately.

What i'm trying to achieve here is making sure the memory is only copied once. And there seem to be a lot of `b''.join(…)` going around to should copy the bytes.

I checked the BytesIO implementation of course, using BytesIO means the copy appends during the "write", so that when we want to access the value, everything is already contiguous.

What I did not know, and is very important here, is that `b''.join(…)` is very optimised for some very specific calls:
```python
>>> b = b'abc'; id(b) == id(b''.join([b]))
True
```
So calling `b''.join([b])` is really just unwinding the input list without copying anything

This is why the bytes don't get copied around too much with the current implementation, without this, everything would get duplicated several times over. A comment mentioning that in streams.py would help.


I'm closing this, I agree the current implementation is optimal:
 - The requests come in chunks, so we have them in memory but not in contiguous parts
 - Storing the request contiguously would require moving the chunks as they come, which is not an option to avoid unnecessarily slowing down everything
 - Since the request is not stored contiguously, getting the body as a single bytestring requires copying things around (this could be done with a bit less memory by releasing the original chunks as they are moved in the result, but this would need to be done in C to have any hope of matching the perfs of `b''.join`, so I won't try it)

Note that currently it doesn't even execute or edit notebooks. So the main functionality of the notebook doesn't actually work *at all*. It's ok to push out alpha tools, but the current state isn't quite at alpha functionality yet, and we run the risk of simply turn users away.

I think we need to introduce it *gradually*. As we gain more functionality, we can display it more prominently, but right now we basically need design/UI feedback, it's not really something people can "try" in the sense of using it as a notebook, even for 5 minutes.

So I think we need:

*  initially a less-prominent invitation, just so it's easy to find it for someone who actually wants to help test.  We can email the list for now to invite people to try it out.  @takluyver's suggestion is a good one. To keep it simple, we could make it  a plain text link for now (to avoid re-design work); once it matures we can use the nice "try me" button.

* an information page that tells people roughly what to expect.  Right now, they might assume that it's an actual notebook, and think the lack of execution (a most basic feature to do anything useful) is missing might be a bug.  We don't want that reported, so people need to know what's to be expected as working and what they should report as a bug.

* as we add features, we can move the invitation to be more prominent, changing the plain text link to be the nicer button at the top.

And yes, I'm a developer (sort of :) who runs off master and tried it, but I was immediately confused by the lack of execution, which I thought was a bug as I would expect that it would at least let me do *something* with the notebooks.  So if *I* had that reaction, we should assume that it can happen to others too and act accordingly.

Oh, and one more:

When we suspend processes in a terminal by pressing Ctrl-Z, that's actually sending SIGTSTP, which has a similar default effect, but can be handled or ignored by the process. I wonder if we should be using that instead of SIGSTOP. It makes the implementation more complex, because you have to somehow check whether pausing the kernel has had any effect, but it may be useful for kernels that know they shouldn't be paused in a certain operation.

Given that it's a new feature, maybe we can keep SIGSTOP (the un-ignorable one) for now, and see if any problems come up, but it's worth thinking about.

[This article](http://www.linusakesson.net/programming/tty/) is an excellent read on things like this.

Hi guys, 
Thanks for the toolbox. 
It already prints a warning when the attack fails but maybe you could document in `base.Attack.__call__` what is the expected output for images who are already misclassified and for failed adversarials (original image and None). 
When the attack fails, having it return None is slightly inconvenient if you are generating a set of adversarial images, which I assume is the main use case for foolbox. For instance, this:
```python
    adversarials = np.empty_like(clean_images)
    for i, (image, label) in enumerate(zip(clean_images, clean_labels)):
        adversarials[i] = attack(image, label)
```
does not fail but fills the failed image attacks with nans. If you are not aware of attack possibly returning None you could think the nans are numerical errors or come from a different source. In fact, in pytorch a nan image can go through the network, preds will be nans and argmax will be zero so if you try to compute accuracy in this set it will work fine but the number will be wrong. If the original image is returned, this will work alright. The workaround is of course easy (just check for None) but it may catch people off-guard.
Cheers,

When we are reading the entity, inside `EntityReadMixin.read()` we're collecting all its fields and entities, placing everything into one resulting entity and returning it.
Why sync_plan makes difference? According to [https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/blob/master/nailgun/entity_mixins.py#L683-L687](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/blob/master/nailgun/entity_mixins.py#L683-L687):
```python
                    referenced_entity = field.entity(
                        self._server_config,
                        id=entity_id,
                    )
                setattr(entity, field_name, referenced_entity)
```
 all sub-entities are initialized by their type and id. It's ok for 99% cases, but for sync_plan we also need to specify organization to initialize it [https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/blob/master/nailgun/entities.py#L3425](https://github.com/SatelliteQE/nailgun/blob/master/nailgun/entities.py#L3425):
```python
 def __init__(self, server_config=None, **kwargs):
        _check_for_value('organization', kwargs)
...
        self._meta = {
            # pylint:disable=no-member
            'api_path': '{0}/sync_plans'.format(self.organization.path()),
        }
```
Long story short, `EntityReadMixin.read()` is trying to access sync plan only by its id, but should access by id and organization.

Sadly we have a couple of overlapping versioning issues and are working on an overall strategy. We're trying to get the right balance of meeting the expectations people have dependencies on (not breaking people) and reducing the confusion. We know we don't have it right. 

`--version` is a challenge. There might be multiple runtimes on the machine. If there is one number output - the SDK is what is running is accurate and there is one single number. But I'm aware that this is not what many people mean to be asking when they type `--version`

I'm currently leaning to a complex result - the SDK version and the installed runtimes. I don't want to turn this into --info, but when a human types `--version` the CLI simply can't know what they want from the request. 

This will break people, so it's a hard call.

@dasa123 Google are in the process of dumping rc4 -

Also there is huge different between using rc4 which will offer a bit less secure connection to small part of your users (users using evergreen browsers without rc4 support are not affected, and even if an rc4 connection is made, a real world attack is hard to implement (best I know is ~50 hours for decrypting a cookie). Compare to your suggestion where XP users by design don't have any security at all, and all a MITM has to do is to drop ssl connections and now any users will not have any secure connection at all.

>*   Modern systems => Allow only https

No, you need to change the umask before creating the file. Something
like

```python

old_umask = os.umask(0o022)
try:
    with open(path, "wx") as validation_file:
        validation_file.write(validation.encode())
finally:
    os.umask(old_umask)
```

This also fails if the file already exists ("x" in open, needs
Python3.3+) and reverts the umask even if an exception is raised.

Since El Capitan 10.11, Apple has created a System Integrity Protection(SIP) that does not even allow a super user to change anything in these folders: /System, /bin, /sbin, or /usr (except /usr /local).

Pre requiremets form MacOS El Capitan or newer:

brew install pkg-config
brew upgrade openssl

echo 'export PATH="/usr/local/opt/openssl/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bash_profile
export LDFLAGS=-L/usr/local/opt/openssl/lib
export CPPFLAGS=-I/usr/local/opt/openssl/include

Because of this, I need clone and compile librdkafka by my own, and changed the prefix fom /usr to /usr/local

git clone https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka.git
cd librdkafka
./configure --clean
./configure --prefix /usr/local 
make
sudo make install

The documentation should be updated now, that assumption of UTC was incorrect on my part - I went through and wrote that documentation aspirationally (e.g. I wrote documentation that I felt matched my intuitions about default values, *then* wrote code for it), but then forgot to remove that part when I realized that if a timezone is specified then it's best to return timezone-naive objects, not UTC objects.

As for allowing the default timezone to be specified, that might be reasonable, but I'm a bit cautious about it because the argument parsing logic is already decently extensive and complicated, I'd rather pruning that back rather than moving it forward.

Depending on how unweildy or quick you want to make your code, there are a few ways to accomplish this, but I think that the easiest thing to do is to just write a wrapper that adds a tzinfo file to a date if none exists:

```python
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>> from dateutil import tz

>>> add_default_tz = lambda x, tzinfo: x.replace(tzinfo=x.tzinfo or tzinfo)

>>> EST = tz.gettz("America/New_York")
>>> parsed_dt = add_default_tz(parse("0:00"), EST)

>>> parsed_dt
datetime.datetime(2015, 8, 22, 0, 0, tzinfo=tzfile('America/New_York'))
```

That said, I think that right now the parser options are kinda spread all over the place - I'm thinking it might be best to consolidate as much as possible into the ``parserinfo`` object. Once this is done, I won't have as many qualms about the complexity of the argument parsing logic because for people running ``parse`` in a loop, the argument parsing overhead can all be moved into the parserinfo construction.

@miigotu Yes, @labrys is right that something like this is the best way to go. I think that in general it's not considered pythonic to aim for different behavior on different platforms, but rather to use a try-and-see duck-typing style approach.

In this case, I'd recommend:

```python
from dateutil import tz
tzlocal = tz.tzwinlocal or tz.tzlocal
```

I don't recommend redefining symbols within dateutil's namespace.

In the end, I'm wondering if maybe we should change the behavior of `tzlocal` such that it defaults to `tzwinlocal` on Windows. I suspect that this may be helpful in issue #57.

We should definitely do step 1 of what Ilai suggested. Step 2 is really a separate matter, since this case is less about the validation_set='auto' and more about allowing users to invoke our validation-set creation code to produce test sets. #969 covers a separate case: the fact that we currently throw an exception if the test set doesn't happen to span all of the labels present in the training data.

With regard to step 2, note that most of our supervised toolkits currently just use the heuristic of using a validation set when the number of instances is above 100. I think the main question here is what is an "instance". A session feels like it's much bigger than an instance, since it may comprise several prediction windows, each of which has a separate (if not independent label). Given that our implementation already splits each session into segments of 20 prediction windows, we could in theory do the auto split if we have at least 100 of these 20-window segments. But that's nontrivial to compute accurately, and is probably best implemented on the C++ side as part of the chunking code. (Chunk first, then split.)

It gives you the position of item X in a list where you ordered all items by how strongly they are recommended (so yes, 42 is 'goodness', and smaller numbers are better, because they are closer to the head of the list). Of course, if you are actually serving recommendations, you should use the `predict` method (which will give you a score where higher is better).

Recommendations are a ranking problem, not a classification problem, so things like thresholds don't really make sense. The idea is that you have, say, 4 slots to fill in your interface, and you sort all products by their recommendation score and pick the top 4; you never set a threshold.

Reading the references is a good start. The [BPR paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/1205.2618) is a classic. There is also [this](http://www.cs.ubbcluj.ro/~gabis/DocDiplome/SistemeDeRecomandare/Recommender_systems_handbook.pdf) but I can't say anything about how good it is.

I chose `newton` specifically because this example uses scalar inputs. The equivalent multivariate solver is [`scipy.optimize.fsolve`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy-0.18.1/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.fsolve.html#scipy.optimize.fsolve) based on [MINPACK](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MINPACK)’s `hybrd` and `hybrj` which is a modified version of [Powell's method](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Powell's_method) and the Newton-Raphson methods.

From the GNU Scientific Library:
>This is a modified version of Powell’s Hybrid method as implemented in the HYBRJ algorithm in MINPACK. Minpack was written by Jorge J. Moré, Burton S. Garbow and Kenneth E. Hillstrom. The Hybrid algorithm retains the fast convergence of Newton’s method but will also reduce the residual when Newton’s method is unreliable. The algorithm uses a generalized trust region to keep each step under control. 

```python
from scipy import optimize
# Three iterations of Newton-Raphson method to solve
# w+log(w)=logargW. The initial guess is w=logargW. Where direct
# evaluation (above) results in NaN from overflow, 3 iterations
# of Newton's method gives approximately 8 digits of precision.
lambertwterm_log = optimize.fsolve(
    func=lambda w: w + np.log(w) - logargW,
    x0=logargW,
    fprime=lambda w: 1 + 1/w)
```

If even more flexibility is needed, then [`scipy.optimize.root`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy-0.18.1/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.root.html#scipy.optimize.root) is the general interface to multivariate equation solvers. You can specify the `method` argument, EG: `lm` is Levenberg-Marquardt for least square fitting of non-square systems of equations.

@cwhanse the equivalent of this in MATLAB would be the Optimization Toolbox's `fsolve` which I re-implemented in [the MATLAB FEX Newton-Raphson solver](https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/fileexchange/43097-newton-raphson-solver)

### Summary of our offline discussion
- IAM Certificates: Support Names with Date suffix
  - The name _multidomain-example-org-comodo_ will find the `multidomain-example-org-comodo` and `multidomain-example-org-comodo-\d{8}`
  - The last one from the sorted list is used from Senza
  - If no name given, then use the default domain (. replaced by -) as name and support the date suffix
- ACM Certificate: Right Certificate is find by the domain entrys and not only the Name (primary domain)

input shape = 7

xxxxxxx0
ww

      ww

Yes, I think we're on the same page; I am not married to a particular implementation, but I would propose that the requirements are (at least):

1) Layers which do not use some "side information" must be able to appear in the layer chain before layers which do use it (not propagating side information would prevent this without some additional mechanism).  I would argue that this is absolutely not a special case - e.g., a convolutional "feature extraction" front-end followed by a recurrent sequence summarization backend is a common idea.
2) Layers which provide multiple outputs must be able to do so without having their multiple outputs overridden (e.g. if we stack two LSTM layers, the user should have access to both the hidden and cell values of both layers, if they want them)
3) There must be a way to tell each layer which "side information" to use (e.g. if we have a network graph where there are two branches each with recurrent layers and their own input layers, we need to be able to use separate masks for each branch)

+ any others stipulated by other use-cases?

> which gives the shape of `(2,)` always

Unfortunately not:
```
>>> x = range(1)
>>> len(x[-2:])
1
```

If the input length on the slices axis is shorter than 2, the output length will also be shorter than 2 (and there is no error). So we can only infer the output length if the input length is known, there is no way around it. Sorry!

If you cannot make the input shape known, a workaround is to use an `ExpressionLayer(layer, lambda X: X[-2:], lambda s: (2,) + s[1:])` (adapt for the axis you need). For a more general solution, we'd need to extend the shape definition to include minimum length guarantees for unknown shapes, but that's overkill.

> What do you think about moving coeffs to a separate layer? a simple layer that scales its input with a constant or a vector?

I think that would make two common use cases more complex:
* ``ElemwiseSumLayer([l1, l2, l3], coeffs=1./3)`` for averaging three layers would become ``ScaleLayer(ElemwiseSumLayer([l1, l2, l3]), 1./3)``.
* ``ElemwiseSumLayer([l1, l2, l3], coeffs=[0.2, 0.5, 0.3])`` for mixing three layers with specific weights would become ``ElemwiseSumLayer([ScaleLayer(l1, 0.2), ScaleLayer(l2, 0.5), ScaleLayer(l3, 0.3)])``.

So I'd still be in favor of moving `coeffs` to `ElemwiseSumLayer`. @benanne, what do you think?

On a different note, it would be good to mention `T.minimum` and `T.maximum` as further possible merge functions in the `ElemwiseMergeLayer` docstring -- people might try `T.min` or `T.max` otherwise.

This is actually a problem that should be solved at the Theano level imo. The only reason we have different classes is because all the Theano backends have different interfaces and support a different subset of features. Once they unify all of these (which afaik they are planning to do, but I don't know when it will happen), we can basically get rid of all of them.

I think the simplest option would be a global kwarg whitelist, and a check in `get_output` to raise if something in `kwargs` is not in this list. 

It would only catch misspellings, not incorrect usage of existing kwargs, but I'm not sure you can really protect against that anyway.

Here's how the classic notebook does it:

* User clicks `New ˅`
* A `GET` for each of Contents (on `/`), Sessions, and Terminals is done
  * The Contents one is notable here because the new notebook is created to be a one-up from the last `Untitled${number}.ipynb
* A `POST` to `/api/contents/Untitled${number}.ipynb` is performed with payload:

```js
{ "type": "notebook" }
```

* Note: :warning: possible race condition possible between when the directory listing is performed and when this new notebook is created. This is probably more likely with multiple notebook servers running at the same location (NFS, EFS backed)
* The user is redirected to `/notebooks/Untitled${number}.ipynb`
* `POST` to `/api/sessions` with:

```js
{
  path: `Untitled4.ipynb`,
  type: "notebook",
  name: "",
  kernel: {
    id: null,
    name: "python3"
  }
}
```

* Server responds with the created session and kernel:

```
{
  id: "35aeee00-79f9-43ad-b674-20618d91adf5",
  path: "Untitled4.ipynb",
  name: "",
  type: "notebook",
  kernel: {
    id: "816af886-ede7-47bd-bc93-6cdcfe7fedd2",
    name: "python3",
    last_activity: "2018-02-14T21:34:49.945834Z",
    execution_state: "starting",
    connections: 0
  },
  notebook: { path: "Untitled4.ipynb", name: "" }
}
```

* Channels are connected to, noting that the `session_id` is not the same as the session's id .____.
* Contents are fetched

I've previously used `bcp` by writing csvs to spinning rust but whilst it does get you very good speedups the whole process felt a bit icky.  In contrast the postgres bulk upload could be used entirely in memory by writing the csv to a `StringIO`. I tried to avoid writing to disk by using a named pipe but couldn't get that working. I've read various things on the internet that suggest there *may* be a way to make that work but I haven't followed that up.

In this instance I've got a bit of `.NET` envy - the [`SqlBulkCopy`](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.data.sqlclient.sqlbulkcopy?view=netframework-4.7) class uses the same apis as `bcp` and so gets the same performance but directly from C# code. I haven't pursued the named pipe route as I think the proper way to do this is to implement the [Tabular Data Stream Protocol](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/cc448435.aspx?f=255&MSPPError=-2147217396) directly in Python as `SqlBulkCopy` does in C#. I think that doable but is a big project which I don't personally have time for at the moment.

***Refs:***
* https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/cc448435.aspx?f=255&MSPPError=-2147217396
* https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/dd340549.aspx?f=255&MSPPError=-2147217396

强烈建议把异步的特性单独成立一个项目，比如 wechatpy-async。对于我们的项目来说，完全用不到异步，但不得不安装一些无用的模块。虽然不用的时候，一般不会产生影响。但我们还是担心会出现意想不到的事情。

**类似的情况也有很多，但都是分开处理的。** 比如 一直有人想让requests支持异步，但requests没有这么做，于是出现了grequests。

**分开处理，保持项目的简单性。** 这在django-rest-framework中体现的最为突出。之前django-rest-framework是支持 xml、csv 等格式的，现在只支持 json，因为这是大部分情况。而把 xml、csv 单独出来，需要的人可以先安装django-rest-framework，再安装django-rest-framework-xml就可以用了。

现在返回数据的方式用着很不方便，以及一些东西显得多余，我想把这个库改造得更面向对象一些，屏蔽掉网络层。这样对调用者更方便。如果这时候还要考虑异步，情况会变得很复杂。

如果实现了更加面向对象的方式，需要异步支持时，也可以用更高层的设计模式方便的实现。

不需要自己去调用 ``fetch_access_token``，如果你需要保存的话，可以用 [SessionStorage](https://github.com/jxtech/wechatpy/tree/master/wechatpy/session)，比如 RedisStorage

```python
from redis import StrictRedis
from wechatpy import WeChatClient
from wechatpy.session.redisstorage import RedisStorage

redis = StrictRedis.from_url('redis_url')
session = RedisStorage(redis, prefix='wechatpy')
client = WeChatClient('appid', 'secret', session=session)
```

或者：

```python
from wechatpy import WeChatClient

client = WeChatClient('appid', 'secret')
access_token = client.access_token
# 然后保存 access_token
```

一般来说用第一种方法就好了，不需要操心 access_token 的问题，直接调用 API，WeChatClient 会自动处理 access_token 的获取和过期更新。

This addresses a specific problem which LHCb users are still afflicted with.
When a user has a large inputdata set (10k+ files) writing this to disk and reading it in is a very expensive OP.

This PR addresses the XML problem by reducing the need to fully construct objects which don't need to be fully qualified and reduces the number of I/O ops for the writing of the XML to disk by using a String buffer which allows for the data to be written in a single bust after being constructed.

Testing with a dummy Job containing 10k DiracFile in the inputfile field:

Without this PR: 70sec +  37sec  = 107sec
With this PR:       54sec + 17sec  =  71sec

~30% performance improvement in the basic test and I claim some general speed improvements in casual use using this PR.

Construction:

%time j=Job(inputfiles=[DiracFile(str(_)+'.root') for _ in range(10000)])

restart ganga with `--no-mon`

Based on some comments on my [StackOverflow cry for help](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45621347/how-can-two-delete-queries-deadlock-in-postgres), it seems like actually, the index *is* being used, and it's guiding the lock acquisition. In #1310, I tried locking them in `ttl` order, and that lead to deadlocks. Locking them according to key (`key`), as suggested by a commenter, leads to no deadlocks. So does locking them according to physical order (`ctid`).

I still don't really understand why locking them according to `ttl` order leads to deadlocks in our application. Any update to a cache entry should remove it from the set that should be about to be deleted. One commenter suggested that ordering by `ttl` is not well-defined if two entries have the same `ttl`, but I haven't been able to reproduce it that way either. But at this point I'm running out of patience -- I have two fixes that both seem to work and a vague understanding, so maybe that's good enough.

Yes, you are right. 
In general adding more texture will help depth. 
There are also other factors to consider, namely that a well textured passive target actually gives best results. 
Here is an example target. 
![random_image_10_95_5_4800_3200](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/134740/37352655-ff72b460-269a-11e8-900a-ba96ae6baf78.png)
Why does it give better results? 
1. There is high spatial frequency content 
2. There is no laser speckle. We found that even after all the precautions taken in to reduce speckle in the D415 and D435, using a passive target can give 30% less depth noise.  

So the upshot is that in some cases it may actually be better to turn off the projector and simply illuminate well with white light. The RealSense cameras actually behave quite well outside in bright sunlight because the natural texture becomes even more visible. The stereo depth will always be limited by the quality of the left and right images. It is well known from cell phone cameras, for example, that they give better images in daylight than in dimly lit indoor environments. The same applies to the small RealSense cameras.

The mapping of `(u,v)` to a color pixel is: 
For `{u/v}` : `[0-1]` --> `[0-{width-1/height-1}]`, Meaning that 
`(0,0)` is mapped to `(0,0)` 
`(0,1)` is mapped to `(0,height-1)` 
`(1,0)` is mapped to `(width-1,0)` 
`(1,1)` is mapped to `(width-1,height-1)` 

We do not provide a function that takes **texture (u, v)** and returns **RGB (r, g, b, a)**, mainly because mostly rendering libraries take texture parameters and will handle it on their own. But you can do the mapping yourself if you wish by converting texture to pixel using the above explanation.

The patch is incompatible with at least Linux 4.4.0-36 and 4.4.0-34.

The patch is trying to add definitions for `V4L2_PIX_FMT_Y8I`, `V4L2_PIX_FMT_Y12I`, and `V4L2_PIX_FMT_Z16` on lines [174](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/scripts/realsense-camera-formats.patch#L174), 175, and 176. However, these definitions already exist in my videodev2.h. `/usr/src/linux-headers-4.4.0-36/include/uapi/linux/videodev2.h` and `/usr/src/linux-headers-4.4.0-34/include/uapi/linux/videodev2.h` contain:
```
#define V4L2_PIX_FMT_JPGL	v4l2_fourcc('J', 'P', 'G', 'L') /* JPEG-Lite */
#define V4L2_PIX_FMT_SE401      v4l2_fourcc('S', '4', '0', '1') /* se401 janggu compressed rgb */
#define V4L2_PIX_FMT_S5C_UYVY_JPG v4l2_fourcc('S', '5', 'C', 'I') /* S5C73M3 interleaved UYVY/JPEG */
#define V4L2_PIX_FMT_Y8I      v4l2_fourcc('Y', '8', 'I', ' ') /* Greyscale 8-bit L/R interleaved */
#define V4L2_PIX_FMT_Y12I     v4l2_fourcc('Y', '1', '2', 'I') /* Greyscale 12-bit L/R interleaved */
#define V4L2_PIX_FMT_Z16      v4l2_fourcc('Z', '1', '6', ' ') /* Depth data 16-bit */

/* SDR formats - used only for Software Defined Radio devices */
#define V4L2_SDR_FMT_CU8          v4l2_fourcc('C', 'U', '0', '8') /* IQ u8 */
```

So to fix the issue, replace lines 170-176 of `scripts/realsense-camera-formats.patch` with:
```
@@ -624,6 +624,19 @@ struct v4l2_pix_format {
 #define V4L2_PIX_FMT_Y8I      v4l2_fourcc('Y', '8', 'I', ' ') /* Greyscale 8-bit L/R interleaved */
 #define V4L2_PIX_FMT_Y12I     v4l2_fourcc('Y', '1', '2', 'I') /* Greyscale 12-bit L/R interleaved */
 #define V4L2_PIX_FMT_Z16      v4l2_fourcc('Z', '1', '6', ' ') /* Depth data 16-bit */```
Rebased and updated the documentation.

Hi all,

First, thanks to everyone for your efforts on this. Also sorry for my delay on this - I've been catching up on things and probably won't be back to normal until next week sometime. I'm sure some of the confusion I'll refer to below is because I wasn't able to review all the changes immediately.

Second, some procedural aspects. I don't usually like to over-specify procedure and prefer to just let people work, but here is an instance where things got really disorganized. The original featurizer_refactor branch from @WardLT and @JFChen3 was a good step at least in terms of organizing changes. However, this branch got overtaken by @matk86 with severe changes that as far as I can tell were not approved or even discussed with the people originating and working in that branch. Please don't do this. If you have your own ideas and just want to work on them without the hassle of discussing them with others, that's fine, but then create your own branch for that. Otherwise it becomes very difficult for me to see the different opinions and also for the original authors to continue with their idea (I also now have yet another PR that seems to be working off the featurizer_refactor branch, but with the original scheme (?) rather than @matk86 's revised scheme - further making this situation complicated). So if you want to go solo, then do it on your own branch and don't start mixing it in with other people's work.

Finally, I haven't had a chance to look at the code in detail. Some minor aspects of @matk86 's change seem fine - e.g., rename "composition_features" to "composition". However, I am not a fan of the refactor in general. Multiple reasons:

* Take SiteAttribute() vs CompositionFeaturizer(). In the former, the data object to be operated on (structure) is in the constructor of the class. In the latter, the data object to be operated on (the composition) is not in the constructor but rather an argument of the featurize() function. This kind of inconsistency is not acceptable. I would say that the each subclass of the base featurizer should represent a particular *descriptor* (or perhaps, a set of related descriptors such as pymatgen elemental descriptors). Then the featurize() method takes in a specific data object (e.g., composition or structure) and returns that feature for that data object. This is also in-line with the original vision of having separate classes for each feature, and perhaps having some of the class attributes be things like a reference/citation for that particular feature. That is, we would *not* create a new class for each new structure or composition to process. As for the specific way in which the base featurizer should look (e.g., whether it takes in a dataframe and column name for the data object or simply just takes the appropriate data object without worrying about dataframes), I haven't thought much about it or decided anything. But the current major difference in usage/ style between different subclasses of AbstractFeaturizer is not OK.
* There is already too many hierarchies of classes and subclasses - it's a lot to take on and understand. Ideally, there is just going to be one base class for a featurizer and all descriptors are just going to subclass that master base class. I could deal with perhaps one more intermediate layer of hierarchy if it seems important, e.g., BaseFeaturizer->CompositionFeaturizer->SpecificFeature and also BaseFeaturizer->StructureFeaturizer->SpecificFeature - only if *really* needed. The current solution has something like this latter case, except that (for example) StructureFeaturizer is an intermediary layer that also has a bunch of specific features mixed into it.
* Some of the terminology is strange, e.g. SiteFeaturizer that actually returns structure features (volume per site is a structure feature, e.g. it is not different for each different site??)

I'll try to actually sort through some of this in more detail hopefully next week. But in the meantime I would like:

* The featurizer_refactor to be restored back to where it was left off by @WardLT and @JFChen3, so I can independently see whether those changes by themselves were a step in a good direction without the further changes 
* Any proposed further changes on top of that to be in a new branch (or, discussed / approved with @WardLT / @JFChen3)

This is a tricky one; it's both a rough edge and the intention to be able to handle this gracefully. We have an implementation of the IntentDeterminationEngine called [DomainIntentDeterminationEngine)[https://github.com/MycroftAI/adapt/blame/master/adapt/engine.py#L171], the goal of which is to allow for the type of vocabulary isolation you're describing. However, it take a lot of interesting interactions off the table.

For example:
```
U: Mycroft, play some music
M: <music plays>
U: Mycroft, what is playing?
M: Awesome, by The Awesome Band of Awesomes
U: Are they playing nearby any time soon?
M: The Awesome Band of Awesomes will be playing at the Big Theater next monday. Would you like me to purchase tickets?
```

To parse the third user utterance, mycroft is relying on entities from the music skill to populate a query to the events/ticketing skills. We're obviously a ways from supporting that type of interaction, but adapt was designed with the goal of supporting just that.

Intent modeling is our crutch at this point to solve problems in this area. They're edge-casey, and typically addressed in an ad-hoc manner. Say for example you want to support

```
Play <song>
and
Play <movie>
```
Your media collection has both a song and a movie named "The Awesomes", and you end up with an ambiguous statement. Here, we'd rely on additional context to solve the problem. 

Ambient context might be "was I just browsing movies or listening to music? Stay within the current domain."
Explicit context might include modeling context vocabulary into the intents, to allow for explicit correction.
```
U: No, I meant play music by the Awesomes
```

Hi @prokofiev!

You can actually use the `default_value` in the Argument and set another default than `None`.

So currently you can do:
```python
class Undefined(object): pass

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    field = graphene.String(
        input=graphene.String(default_value=Undefined)
    )
```

It might be interesting to set `Undefined` as default_value in a `GraphQLArgument` in `graphql-core` instead of `None`, but probably this is more that we have to investigate a little bit further :)

> If we were to follow such principle, then why have debug/info/etc methods in the loggers if we can have one catch-all log(lots-of-args) method? The answer is - for semantic clarity.

I thought the removal of debug/info/error already had [consensus](https://github.com/opentracing/opentracing.github.io/issues/30#issuecomment-173766654) (for now).

And this does nail my argument of plumbing vs porcelain api design.
The plumbing api targets first principles, the porcelain more the semantic clarity.
By dividing them and tackling first principles first everything often is simplified.
By prematurely tacking on the semantic clarify stuff the design can become unfortunately shackled. 

>  - starting a new trace where none exists
>  - joining an existing trace started in another process
>  - stating a child span, aka joining an existing in-process trace
>
> These 3 use cases have different semantics not just for the end user

I'm arguing that they are in fact not.

The end-user does not know, and ultimately should not care, if the span that they are now creating is part of an existing trace or will be initiating a new trace.

You see this in the code examples. If there exists a context (extracted from the protocol's middleware framework) then the end-user always uses it. The code examples are deterministic, and can therefore be simplified.

Having learned a bit more about the internals, I concur that changing the
default behavior was not a suitable request.

Since Python switches automatically from standard integers to long integers
the integer values can get pretty large.
For example, in Python 3.5 on a Linux box:
>> temp = sys.maxsize
>> for i in range(1, 100) temp = temp * sys.maxsize

temp is now a 2068-digit integer.

If you can guide me towards the conversion code I’ll be happy to dig into
it as well.

modified pyfftw.interfaces._utils (header comment omitted):
```
'''
Utility functions for the interfaces routines
'''

import hashlib

def hash(x):
    return hashlib.md5(x).hexdigest()[0:8]

import pyfftw.builders as builders
import pyfftw
import numpy
from . import cache

other_array = None

def _Xfftn(a, s, axes, overwrite_input, planner_effort,
        threads, auto_align_input, auto_contiguous,
        calling_func, normalise_idft=True, ortho=False):


    global other_array

    work_with_copy = False

    print('A1', hash(a), hash(other_array))

    a = numpy.asanyarray(a)

    try:
        s = tuple(s)
    except TypeError:
        pass

    try:
        axes = tuple(axes)
    except TypeError:
        pass

    if calling_func in ('irfft2', 'irfftn'):
        # overwrite_input is not an argument to irfft2 or irfftn
        args = (planner_effort, threads, auto_align_input, auto_contiguous)

        if not overwrite_input:
            # Only irfft2 and irfftn have overwriting the input
            # as the default (and so require the input array to
            # be reloaded).
            work_with_copy = True
    else:
        args = (overwrite_input, planner_effort, threads,
                auto_align_input, auto_contiguous)

        if not a.flags.writeable:
            # Special case of a locked array - always work with a
            # copy.  See issue #92.
            work_with_copy = True

            if overwrite_input:
                raise ValueError('overwrite_input cannot be True when the ' +
                                 'input array flags.writeable is False')

    if work_with_copy:
        # We make the copy before registering the key so that the
        # copy's stride information will be cached since this will be
        # used for planning.  Make sure the copy is byte aligned to
        # prevent further copying
        a_original = a
        a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(shape=a.shape, dtype=a.dtype)
        a[...] = a_original

    if cache.is_enabled():
        key = (calling_func, a.shape, a.strides, a.dtype, s.__hash__(),
               axes.__hash__(), args)

        try:
            if key in cache._fftw_cache:
                FFTW_object = cache._fftw_cache.lookup(key)
            else:
                FFTW_object = None

        except KeyError:
            # This occurs if the object has fallen out of the cache between
            # the check and the lookup
            FFTW_object = None

    if not cache.is_enabled() or FFTW_object is None:

        # If we're going to create a new FFTW object and are not
        # working with a copy, then we need to copy the input array to
        # preserve it, otherwise we can't actually  take the transform
        # of the input array! (in general, we have to assume that the
        # input array will be destroyed during planning).
        if not work_with_copy:
            a_copy = a.copy()

        planner_args = (a, s, axes) + args

        FFTW_object = getattr(builders, calling_func)(*planner_args)

        # Only copy if the input array is what was actually used
        # (otherwise it shouldn't be overwritten)
        if not work_with_copy and FFTW_object.input_array is a:
            a[:] = a_copy

        print('A2', hash(a), hash(other_array))

        if cache.is_enabled():
            cache._fftw_cache.insert(FFTW_object, key)

        output_array = FFTW_object(normalise_idft=normalise_idft, ortho=ortho)

        print('A3', hash(a), hash(other_array))
    else:

        print('A4', hash(a), hash(other_array))

        orig_output_array = FFTW_object.output_array
        output_shape = orig_output_array.shape
        output_dtype = orig_output_array.dtype
        output_alignment = FFTW_object.output_alignment

        output_array = pyfftw.empty_aligned(
            output_shape, output_dtype, n=output_alignment)

        print('A5', hash(a), hash(other_array))

        FFTW_object(input_array=a, output_array=output_array,
                normalise_idft=normalise_idft, ortho=ortho)

        print('A6', hash(a), hash(other_array))

    return output_array
```

I don't see this as a very common feature, but however could be cleanly implemented using a class of "column selectors":

- By default, if the first element of the feature definition is an string or a list, select those columns.
- If the first element is an instance of subclass of `ColumnSelector`, use `column_selector.select(dataframe)`.

Default implementation:
```
class ColumnSelector:
    def __init__(self, columns):
        self.columns = columns

    def select(self, dataframe):
        return dataframe[self.columns]
```
This way one can easily define subclasses of this class like:

```
class ExcludeColumnsSelector(ColumnSelector):
    def __init__(self, columns_to_exclude):
        self.columns_to_exclude = columns_to_exclude

    def select(self, dataframe):
        columns = dataframe.columns - self.columns_to_exclude
        return dataframe[self.columns]
``` 

And use them like:
```
mapper = DataFrameMapper([
    (ExcludeColumnsSelector('a'), LabelBinarizer())
])
```

Opinions?

We consider it  good practice to use `unique_ptr` when you can and `shared_ptr` when you need to (on the same class), and having to inform downstream callers which holder was used so they can know when to wrap a smart-pointer-converting lambda instead of the original function is a workaround we'd like to avoid.

Our organization may be able to contribute some effort towards fixing this, and the `holder_conversion` proposal above sounds quite reasonable - I think it would *have* to be a runtime-only operation, since code returning a `*_ptr<T>` doesn't know anything about the `py::class_<T...>` instantiation that was used.  But it sounds like that would also have to involve adding some new weight to possibly performance-critical places, and I'd like to have some agreement that a change in that direction would not be dead-on-arrival before diving in. As far as I can see, the new negative refcount issue has to do with types derived in Python:
```python
class PyDog(m.Dog):
    pass

instance = PyDog("Molly")
del instance  # `m.Dog` is decremented twice
```

Looks like the issue is [here in `subtype_dealloc()`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/8621bb5d93239316f97281826461b85072ff6db7/Objects/typeobject.c#L1248-L1254) which is the `dealloc` used by types created in Python. It calls `basedealloc(self)` and then `Py_DECREF(type)`. But since `basedealloc == pybind11_object_dealloc`, it calls another `Py_DECREF(type)` and we end up with a negative refcount.

The solution could be to replace the `Py_DECREF(type)` in `pybind11_object_dealloc` with:
```c++
if (type->tp_dealloc == pybind11_object_dealloc)
    Py_DECREF(type);
```

Unfortunately, while this does fix everything in `test_class`, I get additional errors in `test_sequences_and_iterators` and `test_virtual_functions`, so there's more to this.
I think that all the access methods should be like this on the c++ side (except for `at`)  which according to the STL idoms (e.g. std::vector) is checked.

It's that `requests` does `from .packages.urllib3.contrib import pyopenssl` and urllib3's pyopenssl module does merely `import six` rather than the proper, vendored version. Thus, it's at the mercy of whatever version of six you have floating around in your Python environment, if any. It's still that way in the latest `requests` and `urllib3`. I'll file a bug.

certbot-auto installs should not be have this problem, because we pin six ourselves as part of dependency-requirements.txt.

I'll try to get this fixed fast upstream so we don't have to burn energy working around it in our OS packages.

(I'm still not sure why six has anything to do with the import failure.)

> I built this PR on top of #4694, so I was intending it to work correctly with these changes. Manual testing indicates that's the case.

It works, but it's a new flag that we have a chance to modify right now. Does it work the way we want long term. To be fair, this may not be the right place for this discussion as I don't think it should block this PR, however, it may cause us to block shipping #4694.

With that said, after thinking about it, I'm personally fine with things as they are now. Given I think we should support a flag like this, I think our options are:

1. Create both IPv4 and IPv6 versions of each flag.
2. Have one flag that can work for either address type.

Having separate flags makes Certbot uglier and increases complexity (what happens if you provide both IPv4 and IPv6 flags?). Currently, Certbot just works whether you provide an IPv4 or IPv6 address on the command line. As other plugins support this flag, we may have to write code that determines whether an address is IPv4 or IPv6 but then every plugin can benefit from it.

The only downside I see to this approach is you can't tell Certbot to listen on both IPv4 and IPv6 and specify the interface for each one. The only reason I could come up with where someone would want to do this is they have multiple domains that each require a different interface be used but they want all domains in the same cert. This is an extreme edge case though and I don't think it's unreasonable to have the user create two certs in this case.

I'd like someone else to make sure they agree with me here, but otherwise, I think the only thing left to worry about here are my obnoxious nits.

We don't recommend you use Certbot on macOS. Support on that platform has always been experimental. If you don't include the `--debug` flag on the command line when you try to install Certbot on that system, you get a message like:
```
FATAL: macOS support is very experimental at present...
if you would like to work on improving it, please ensure you have backups
and then run this script again with the --debug flag!
Alternatively, you can install OS dependencies yourself and run this script
again with --no-bootstrap.
```
Instead, we recommend you follow our macOS instructions found at https://certbot.eff.org/#osx-other. Our instructions recommend you use Homebrew, but if you prefer MacPorts, there is a [port](https://www.macports.org/ports.php?by=name&substr=certbot) available.

To follow up from the meeting's discussion: I looked into the source code for the typing module (https://hg.python.org/cpython/file/3.5/Lib/typing.py). It turns out that the implementation of these values are quite complex. However, you should be able to use special attributes (marked with double underscores) to access data. E.g., for lists:

```python
>>> list_type = List[int]
>>> list_type.__origin__
typing.List  # has some other stuff you can ignore.
>>> list_type.__origin__ == List
True
>>> list_type.__args__
(<class 'int'>,)
```

Use `dir` on these type values to see what attributes they have, and do some investigation to see how to get them.

I was considering that. My thinking here was that, because openssl releases more frequently than python, it would likely be best from a security perspective to allow for system openssl use across the board instead of only < 2.7.9.

That said, it can also have adverse effects should the user's system openssl be older than Python's (hopefully never the case, but I'm sure it's bound to happen). Perhaps a disclaimer in the README about ensuring an up to date system openssl would be the best way to mitigate any backwards incompatibility issues that crop up due to the change?

To clarify, flask has a different policy then Raiden, instead of crashing on uncaught exceptions they are printed and the server continues serving.

In Raiden we must handle the recoverable exceptions or crash the node. This means the [logic used in flask](https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/dfd3619d6f8796d48fc4e32f819cec9e8aa59156/flask/app.py#L1801-L1816) must be tweaked. Possible fixes:

- Install a error handler that will re-raise the exception
- Overwrite the [handle_user_exception](https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/dfd3619d6f8796d48fc4e32f819cec9e8aa59156/flask/app.py#L1680) to always raise exceptions
- Overwite the [full_dispatch_event](https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/dfd3619d6f8796d48fc4e32f819cec9e8aa59156/flask/app.py#L1801-L1816) and completely skip the exception handlers.

I would go with option 1, because there may be use internal flask exceptions that we are unaware of.

I see. Unfortunately, I believe this is just a fundamental limitation of CloudWatch Logs and Lambda. 

See for example https://forums.aws.amazon.com/thread.jspa?threadID=178855. 

Pulumi has no role in or ability to impact the creation of LogStreams. That is entirely managed by Lambda and CloudWatch Logs. 

Barring AWS making improvements here, you may want to do some periodic cleanup to ensure that Logs cans be queried efficiently.

We should also make sure that the approach `pulumi Logs` takes to performing the CloudWatch Logs queries is as optimal as possible in he face of a large number of log streams - though I suspect this query performance degradation is also a limitation of CloudWatch Logs.

How about adding "aliases: [...]" to the general resource options? Then a rename would be:

1) move the current name in to the aliases option array.
2) provide a new primary name.
3) run `pulumi update`
4) later, remove the alias.

This approach means that the rename has a trail in source control where both names exist at once.

As a bonus, code that still uses the old name wouldn't immediately break, but could issue a warning about using a deprecated / non-primary resource name.

This has been asked in #105 and there's also a PR for this: #920. 

Right now you can use the `-O` parameter and save the additional bandwidth like this:
```
streamlink -O url stream | tee recording.ts | vlc - 
```
This uses the standard output, pipes it into the tee process, which writes its input into a file and also outputs it again and pipes it into the vlc process which again reads from stdin.

Users having to check dimension names and specify units can save them a lot of work when they find out later the units aren't what they were expecting, or the dimensions are ordered differently than they were expecting. I'd say it's a good feature for us to have.

What I mean is that a component from another model has to specify its own initialization routines for quantities, which can't take advantage of our infrastructure for broadcasting dimensions. Maybe we can fix this by integrating initialization into Sympl and having registration of initializers.

A compromise may be to have this be an option for get_grid. We should also write all of our components so that they will work even when the initial arrays are not "full". This way we're only restricting our own code, and not user code. This allows users to write their own initialization code that uses non-full initial arrays, and it will work with our code.

I still may agree with you in the end about just having get_grid return full arrays, but I want to keep arguing this out to make sure we're making the right choice.

> Hello, how is possible to change the theme of all the ipywidgets? Thank you!

In JupyterLab (which is designed to be themed), the ipywidgets theme variables you can set in your theme are:

https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/blob/master/packages/controls/css/widgets-base.css#L14-L53

As you can see, we get the font color straight from the JupyterLab theme. So change the color in the jlab theme, and it automatically changes for all widgets.

For the classic notebook, which is not designed for easy theming, we have to compile down to specific colors for backwards compatibility, so it's *much* more difficult (i.e., you would have to distribute your own widgets package with your own compiled css, or you would have to find all places fonts were used and insert appropriate css on the page).

I think that this would work well as a core widget b/c it leverages basic HTML/javascript properties without having to add any more outside javascript libraries. Also I think this widget should be easy for beginners to use. If it were maintained as a separate package then that makes it that much harder for them.

Great question. services (and all of the jlab packages) now compile to es2015 instead of es5, but widgets in the classic notebook still supports browsers that don't support es2015. Lucky for us, though, we don't use any new advanced features of services, so actually in the classic notebook, services 1 or 2 or 3 is just fine for us. However, for JupyterLab, we need to keep up to the latest release.

For widgets 8, we'll probably drop support for older browsers, and then will upgrade to services 3 across the board.

Hmm, ignore all my comments about `set_coroutine_wrapper` above, that's obviously useless because it only runs after the coroutine object is created (or not).

On the overall "is this useful" discussion: I guess I'll reiterate that where this all started is that I had a note that "hmm we should think about whether/how to propose something here", and I think that's still true :-)

I'm actually a bit frustrated at the argument that there are other places where Python can also let mistakes pass silently, because first, surely that just means we should think if we can fix those too [1]. And second, obviously this is a question of trade-offs: the context manager issue is fairly rare and not a big deal; OTOH it would be intolerable if Python silently accepted things like function calls where an argument was inadvertently omitted. Pointing out that one end of this spectrum exists doesn't tell us where `await` falls on it.

My personal suspicion is that we'll eventually come to consensus that the right conceptual model for `await` is as a special kind of function call, rather than the Future-based model that was inherited from C# and then sorta-halfway-rejected in the move to a `yield from`-based design. But this is only a prediction. It's based on some careful thought, but whether it's actually true or not is something we'll gradually learn more about over the next few months/years... and as we do then our perspective on these trade-offs may well change. In the mean time I'm happy to leave this issue here for now :-)
