> Getting a unused port and keeping it this way

I wonder if we should make a `closed_port` fixture or something like that? The most reliable way to make sure a port is unused is to `bind` a socket to it and then not call `listen` – that way you're not listening, but you're also guaranteed that no-one else can claim the port and start listening. BUT, MacOS has some really screwy behavior where if you try to connect to a port that has nothing bound, it fails instantly, while if you try to connect to a port that's bound but not listening, it waits 30 seconds or so before failing. So a `closed_port` fixture would want to the reliable thing on Linux/Windows (bind a port and don't listen), and the theoretically-slightly-less-reliable-but-it-actually-works thing on MacOS (pick an unused port but don't bind it).

Or maybe this is overthinking things :-)

> improve the listener object to easily expose the tcp port

Yeah, we should definitely do this, for lots of reasons – it's actually got an issue open for it already: https://github.com/python-trio/trio/issues/280

I guess this is the other advantage of removing `unused_tcp_port`, it forces us to think of more specific solutions to these problems :-).

All that said, I'm not *really* bothered by the unused tcp port pattern; the theoretical race condition bugs me a bit, but in practice like... every networking library test suite uses it and it works fine in practice. I mostly don't want people to be using the more awkward approaches just because they don't realize that trio has alternatives.

Thinking about it more, I'm realizing that part of what's bugging me here is just that the use of fixtures for this is kind of awkward. The port ends up with a long name, there's no way to configure behavior (e.g. right now it has a baked in assumption that you're always going to be using the port for IPv4, not IPv6, and for TCP, not UDP), etc. Maybe what we should do is add a `trio.testing.unused_port` function, so you do:
```python
from trio.testing import unused_port

async def some_test():
    tcp_port = unused_port()
    udp_port = unused_port(type=trio.socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
    ...
```
And then the docs for this function can point out the alternatives?

Anyway, it does sound for like we agree that dropping the fixtures from pytest-trio is a good idea, at least for now, so I'll make a PR :-)

Thank you for your kind words!

I've been able to reproduce the problem using the v3 API. The error in the server logs is quite explicit: `ActiveRecord::UnknownAttributeError (unknown attribute 'build_events' for ProjectHook.)`. Since it used to work I guess it's a Gitlab regression. The problem doesn't seem to exist in the v4 API though.

I don't think the GitLab team will spend a lot of time fixing v3 problems since it's been deprecated and will be completely removed in a few months. Since you continuously upgrade your GitLab installation I'd recommend starting to use the v4 API.

If you do so please have a look at issue #274, the python-gitlab API is changing a bit for v4.

@APMonitor, here is a Python script I created that will take in 2-D data and use 1-D splines to interpolate in 2 dimensions. 

```
# Use 1-D splines to interpolate in 2-D

# Notes:
# Assumes a rectangular grid.
# For best results, ensure the data points have equivalent precision across both dimensions.
# Author: Nathaniel Gates

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.interpolate import CubicSpline

# Create data
x = np.arange(-5, 5, 0.5)
y = np.arange(-5, 5, 1.0)
X, Y = np.meshgrid(x, y)
R = np.sqrt(X**2 + Y**2)
Z = np.sin(R)

# Use the dimension with the most data points to build new splines.
xlen = len(x)
ylen = len(y)
if xlen >= ylen: # Finer resolution along x axis; build additional splines in the x direction
    use_x = True
else: # Finer resolution along y axis; build additional splines in the y direction
    use_x = False

# Initialize plotting
fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.gca(projection='3d')    

# Build splines in y direction
ys = {}
for i in range(xlen):
    ys[i] = CubicSpline(y, Z[:,i])
    ax.plot(X[:,i], y, ys[i](y), color = 'C0')
ax.plot(X[:,i], y, ys[i](y), color = 'C0', label='Splines in y direction')

# Build splines in x direction
xs = {}
for i in range(ylen):
    xs[i] = CubicSpline(x, Z[i,:])
    ax.plot(x, Y[i,:], xs[i](x), color = 'C1')
ax.plot(x, Y[i,:], xs[i](x), color = 'C1', label='Splines in x direction')

# Build additional splines to interpolate to inner points. Use whichever spline has finer discretization.
# Generate additional points
npts = 30
np.random.seed(0)
xrand = np.random.rand(npts)*(x.max()-x.min())+x.min()
yrand = np.random.rand(npts)*(y.max()-y.min())+y.min()

# Build splines, evaluate and plot the points
nval = np.zeros(npts)
for i in range(npts):
    xp = xrand[i]
    yp = yrand[i]
    if use_x == True: # Use splines that run in the x direction
        zn = np.zeros_like(x)
        for j in range(xlen):
            zn[j] = ys[j](yp)
        ns = CubicSpline(x, zn)
        zp = ns(xp)
        nval[i] = zp
    else: # Use splines that run in the y direction
        zn = np.zeros_like(y)
        for j in range(ylen):
            zn[j] = xs[j](xp)
        ns = CubicSpline(y, zn)
        zp = ns(yp)
        nval[i] = zp
    ax.plot([xp], [yp], [zp], 'o', color = 'C3')
ax.plot([xp], [yp], [zp], 'o', color = 'C3', label='Interpolated points')

# Make legend, set axes labels
ax.legend()
ax.set_xlabel('X')
ax.set_ylabel('Y')
ax.set_zlabel('Z')
ax.view_init(elev=20, azim=-35)

plt.show()
```

So you'll create a new Image class that retains the ability to have arbitrary (integer) boundaries and knows its bounds?  I think this is really needed since most of our galaxies will want to be centered near (0,0). 

If you strip out the header, you might want to have some pixel step or size member in the Image class itself to replace the DX and DK elements currently used.  In this case we can also save the scale and origin location in FITS format using the CRPIX / CRVAL keywords for a rudimentary WCS.

You can eliminate other parts of the Image interface that are not being used: the subimages (would not work gracefully with the python arrays probably anyway) and also all the iterators.  But it would be nice to keep the arithmetic overloads.
